quoteFirst: Here every foundling finds its lost mamma; Each rogue, repentant, melts his stern papa; Misers relent, the spendthrift's debts are paid, The cheats are taken in the traps they laid;
quoteLast: the troubles all are past Till the fifth act comes right side up at last, When the young couple, old folks, rogues, and all, Join hands, so happy at the curtain's fall.

quoteLast: ; a beautiful starlight night, with no moon, and so not very light. The sea was as calm as a pond, just a gentle heave as the boat dipped up and down in the swell; an ideal night, except for the bitter cold, for anyone who had to be out in the middle of the Atlantic ocean in an open boat. And if ever there was a time when such a night was needed, surely it was now, with hundreds of people, mostly women and children, afloat hundreds of miles from land.
title: Sinking of the Titanic and Great Sea Disasters
author: Marshall, Logan, 1884-1937 [Editor]

quoteFirst: A flock of sheep that leisurely pass by,
quoteLast: ; the sound of rain, and bees   Murmuring; the fall of rivers, winds and seas,   Smooth fields, white sheets of water, and pure sky;   I've thought of all by turns; and still I lie   Sleepless; and soon the small birds' melodies   Must hear, first utter'd from my orchard trees;   And the first Cuckoo's melancholy cry.
title: Poems in Two Volumes, Volume 1

quoteFirst: Of breaker issued from the belt, and still     Grew with the growing note, and when the note     Had reach’d a thunderous fulness, on those cliffs     Broke, mixt with awful light (the same as that     Living within the belt) whereby she saw     That all those lines of cliffs were cliffs no more,     But huge cathedral fronts of every age,     Grave, florid, stern, as far as eye could see,
quoteLast: : and then the great ridge drew,     Lessening to the lessening music, back,     And past into the belt and swell’d again     Slowly to music: ever when it broke     The statues, king or saint or founder fell;     Then from the gaps and chasms of ruin left     Came men and women in dark clusters round,     Some crying, ‘Set them up! they shall not fall!’
title: Alfred Tennyson
author: Lang, Andrew, 1844-1912

quoteLast: of the 13th the _Southampton_ sighted a large steamer proceeding slowly eastwards.
title: The Invasion of 1910, with a full account of the siege of London
